title: Other Accounting Services

narrative.executive_view: 541219 is not a payroll-bureau code: Census excludes businesses providing payroll services only and instead includes non-CPA accountant, bookkeeper, and billing offices [S1]. I score bookkeeping/write-up and monthly-close work as the dominant operating thesis. AI can remove substantial production labor, but native ledger automation and Intuit's competing managed-bookkeeping offer make the likely outcome a pressured, not a durable, roll-up proposition.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The core service is transaction entry, categorization, reconciliation, accounts payable/receivable processing, and management reporting—not payroll remittance. Intuit's Live service itself includes bank connections, categorization, and reconciliation [S2]. In the related accounting-professional survey, data entry/processing was the leading AI client-service use case (50%) and invoicing/payments the leading internal use case (44%) [S3]. Human review, cleanup of messy source data, client explanations, and controller-level judgment remain meaningful.
narrative.value_capture: Monthly fixed-fee bookkeeping can initially retain hours saved, but its price umbrella is unusually exposed: Intuit sells ongoing Live Bookkeeping at $200, $400, or $600 per month after a $500 cleanup/onboarding month, based on client expense volume [S2]. That is a visible national benchmark for the same recurring close/reconciliation work. I therefore assign modest distribution retention and a weak moat: switching friction and trust help an incumbent, while a zero-licensure core and platform-owned ledger constrain price and client ownership.
narrative.adoption_timing: The accounting-professional survey reports 46% daily AI use and 95% process automation, but it covers a broader, tech-forward accounting population rather than 541219 firms and measures tool/process usage rather than 50% effective replacement of the target's work [S3]. I do not convert those figures into a claim that this code has already crossed the rubric's effective-adoption threshold. A 30% current-effective-adoption estimate and three-year t50 are a cautious bridge from rapid embedded workflow rollout, with the timing score explicitly low confidence.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The frozen screen's 531 $1-10M EBITDA candidates are a mechanical size proxy, not a validated local-bookkeeping deal universe; it is concentrated in 100+ employee classes while most code establishments are much smaller. I found no reopened, code-matched evidence of active add-on consolidators, owner-age share, or a succession shortage, so the buyability score intentionally receives no consolidator or succession credit. The book can be transferable when staff and client records survive the seller, but this packet does not turn that judgment into a scored ownership fact.
narrative.terminal_demand: Paid demand should survive for cleanup, controls, exception resolution, client communication, and higher-value outsourced-controller support. However, the most repeatable portion of the dominant service—monthly categorization, reconciliation, and reporting—is both automated inside the ledger and offered as managed service by the ledger owner [S2]. That creates a different channel from labor savings: the platform can eliminate or reprice the independent firm's product. I classify terminal demand as PRESSURED rather than MELTING because the residual work is real but narrower and less standardized.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: This catch-all code also contains billing offices, medical billing/payment services, non-CPA accounting, and some bundled tax/payroll work; their credentials, client stickiness, and AI exposure differ materially [S1]. The frozen 4-digit 5412 wage mix is CPA-heavy and understates bookkeeper/biller concentration, while the labor and target-count datasets are fixed inputs rather than re-estimated here. There is no accessible, size-matched 541219 transaction set or owner-demographic census in the reopened sources, so multiples, owner age, and consolidation are deliberately low-confidence estimates rather than false precision.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau NAICS 541219 definition
sources.what_it_supports: 541219 excludes tax-preparation-only and payroll-only services and includes non-CPA accountant, bookkeeper, and billing offices.

sources.title: QuickBooks Live Bookkeeping monthly pricing and service description
sources.what_it_supports: Current $500 first-month cleanup/onboarding price; $200/$400/$600 ongoing monthly tiers; and managed categorization, reconciliation, reporting, and bookkeeping assistance.

sources.title: 2025 Intuit QuickBooks Accountant Technology Survey
sources.what_it_supports: Survey of 700 accounting professionals reporting 46% daily AI use, 95% process automation, AI use in data entry/processing, and workflow use in invoicing/payments.

sources.title: 2026 Intuit QuickBooks AI Impact Report
sources.what_it_supports: Broad SMB context: 34,000-plus owner responses and anonymized data from 5.3 million QuickBooks businesses; regular AI use is broad but paid commitment is narrower, and bookkeeping is among top AI task areas.

labor_share.method: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $29,298.0M / sectoral output (T30) $36,927.9M, year 2022
sources: BLS Industry Productivity and Costs flat files (ip.data.1.AllData), measures L02 and T30

occupations.title: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $116,910 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541219); class <5: 35,754 firms, avg revenue/firm $191,355 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,947 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5,777 firms, avg revenue/firm $656,548 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $102,750 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,081 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,497,395 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $234,342 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,247 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,177,727 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $653,814 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 282 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,787,344 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,157,719 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 249 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,583,161 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,847,265 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 531
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Bookkeeping/write-up and billing workflows are task-automatable, while review, cleanup, exception handling, and relationship work remain. The frozen 5412 role mix is a CPA-heavy proxy, so the residual judgment reflects the prompt's bookkeeping/billing-dominant hypothesis rather than a claimed measured wage mix.
caveats: No 541219-specific occupational wage census was reopened.
The supplied role mix is 4-digit 5412 and misstates the bookkeeper/biller concentration of the selected subsegment.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Non-CPA accounting production can be assisted, but review and client judgment remain.
Categorization, transaction entry, reconciliation, and close support are core automation targets [S2].
Tax preparation is adjacent rather than dominant and retains client-specific interpretation.
Reporting and analysis are assisted, while control and advisory accountability remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, document chasing, and routine communications automate readily.
Owner management and client retention are only partly automatable.
General office data handling and follow-up are workflow-automation targets.
AI can prepare analysis, but operating recommendations require judgment.
Billing, statements, and invoice workflows are highly structured [S3].

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Supervision remains necessary for exceptions and quality control.
Residual is treated as bookkeeper/billing-heavy under the disclosed code-specific hypothesis, tempered for cleanup and review work.

pi_dist.rationale: Fixed monthly fees can retain part of hours saved, but transparent native-platform pricing and widespread automation limit durable pass-through.
caveats: QuickBooks Live is a national platform benchmark, not an independent 541219 price census.

pi_moat.rationale: Incumbent trust and messy-books knowledge help, but the dominant core has no CPA licensure moat and the platform owns the ledger and managed alternative.
caveats: Some non-CPA accounting and medical-billing subsegments have different moats.

t50_years.rationale: Rapid workflow adoption supports a near-term path, but daily AI use and process automation are not the rubric's 50% effective task-replacement metric for 541219; three years is a judgmental bridge from the disclosed current 30% estimate.
caveats: No 541219 census measures effective AI replacement.
This is intentionally positive because current effective adoption is below 50%.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Broader surveys show heavy tool use, but their definitions do not establish that half of the selected industry's production work is effectively automated; 30% is a conservative effective-adoption estimate, not a restatement of survey usage.
caveats: S3 covers accounting professionals, not code-matched non-CPA bookkeeping firms.
S4 covers SMB clients across sectors, not providers.

historical_analogs.value: Embedded ledger workflow automation is spreading from assistance and review toward recurring transaction-processing tasks, while paid commitment remains narrower than regular AI use.
historical_analogs.rationale: The sources document current process/tool adoption and the gap between regular use and paid commitment; they do not provide a dated 541219 adoption curve.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Adoption may accelerate or stall with trust, accuracy, and liability concerns.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No reopened owner-level age evidence matches non-CPA bookkeeping/write-up firms, so this is a neutral low-confidence planning assumption and receives no succession bonus.
caveats: Not evidence of an owner-age census.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No code-matched, reopened documentation establishes a succession shortage.

active_consolidators.rationale: No reopened platform-by-platform evidence establishes active 541219 bookkeeping/write-up add-on consolidators within the required window; assign no buyability credit rather than recycle payroll, CPA, or VC evidence.
caveats: Does not assert that no acquisitions occurred; it records no qualifying documented evidence in this packet.

buy_mult.rationale: A conservative normalized EBITDA planning value is used only to avoid false precision: no reopened, accessible 541219 $1-10M EBITDA transaction set supplies a defensible SDE-to-EBITDA normalization.
caveats: Small practice listings often quote revenue or SDE with owner compensation inside.

exit_mult.rationale: A modest scale premium is a low-confidence scenario assumption, not a claimed $10M-plus EBITDA 541219 comparable; platform price pressure and weak consolidation evidence constrain it.
caveats: No reopened exit comp matches the rubric's $10M-plus EBITDA and selected-service scope.

pricing_structure.value: Recurring monthly fixed fees, frequently tiered by bookkeeping volume or expenses, with cleanup/onboarding work separately priced.
pricing_structure.rationale: Intuit's Live Bookkeeping publishes a cleanup/onboarding first-month fee and ongoing monthly expense-tier pricing.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Independent firms may also use hourly, monthly, or hybrid billing.
caveats: Observed pricing is a platform benchmark, not a census of independent firms.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Task automation is high, but current effective adoption is deliberately below 50% and t50 is positive; adoption speed is supported by workflow evidence rather than ownership or consolidation.

terminal_value.justification: Cleanup, review, and advisory remain paid work, but the ledger platform automates and sells the recurring bookkeeping core [S2].

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Payroll-only, CPA-only, tax-only, and generic SMB evidence is not used to claim 541219 ownership, consolidation, or transaction facts; broad accounting/SMB sources are labeled as adoption/task proxies.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: The $200/$400/$600 QuickBooks Live tiers are modeled in capture via pi_dist and pi_moat, not as terminal-demand preservation or adoption evidence.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied 4-digit 5412 role mix is CPA-heavy, whereas the selected 541219 thesis is bookkeeping/write-up and billing; payroll-only businesses are outside the code.
sensitivity: V can be overstated or understated because the residual wage share is a disclosed bookkeeper/biller hypothesis.
Frozen n_band may overrepresent large outsourced-delivery firms rather than local bookkeepers.

subsegments: non-CPA accounting and outsourced controller support
market_boundary.selection_basis: Census includes bookkeeper and billing offices in 541219 while excluding payroll-only providers [S1]; prompt hypothesis selects bookkeeping/write-up as dominant, but no revenue breakout was reopened.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 10.0 at one year to 1.67 at six years; it is 3.33 at the selected three-year midpoint.
V remains capped at 10 across this range because the frozen labor-share input is 0.7934.
M is highly scenario-dependent; selected values produce a modest 0.67 score, not a sourced market claim.

reviewer_note: Remediation attempt 2 corrects the material scope failure: 541219 is researched as other accounting/bookkeeping/write-up/billing, not payroll-only 541214. Every score-driving cited page was reopened on 2026-07-21; ownership, consolidator, and multiple evidence remains intentionally uncredited where code-matched support was not available.
